title: 2026 Toyota GR86 Gets More Power, Revised Suspension
excerpt: Toyota's beloved sports car receives a bump to 240hp and retuned dampers for sharper handling. The lightweight RWD coupe continues to be the enthusiast's choice.
content:
Toyota has officially unveiled the 2026 GR86, and the changes are exactly what enthusiasts have been asking for. Power from the naturally aspirated 2.4-liter flat-four climbs to 240 horsepower, a meaningful 12hp bump over the outgoing model, achieved through revised intake manifold geometry and an updated ECU calibration.

The suspension receives equally significant attention. Toyota Gazoo Racing engineers spent months at the Nurburgring refining the damper tuning, resulting in a setup that offers noticeably improved body control without sacrificing the compliant ride quality that made the original so livable as a daily driver. Spring rates remain unchanged, but the new monotube rear shocks provide a wider range of effective damping.

Inside, the cabin gains a revised 8-inch touchscreen with wireless Apple CarPlay and Android Auto, along with new Ultrasuede-trimmed sport seats that provide better lateral support during spirited driving. The iconic analog tachometer remains front and center, a nod to the purist ethos that defines this car.

Pricing starts at $30,995 for the base model and $34,495 for the Premium trim, making it one of the last genuinely affordable rear-wheel-drive sports cars on the market. First deliveries are expected in late April.

title: Porsche Confirms Electric Cayman for 2027 with 600hp
excerpt: The next-gen Cayman goes fully electric with dual motors, 600hp, and a sub-3.5s 0–60. Porsche promises it'll still feel like a driver's car.
content:
Porsche has pulled back the curtain on the next-generation Cayman, and as expected, it is going fully electric. Built on a new dedicated EV sports car platform developed in-house at Weissach, the electric Cayman will produce a combined 600 horsepower from its dual-motor setup and sprint from 0 to 60 mph in under 3.5 seconds.

But raw acceleration figures only tell part of the story. Porsche chassis engineers have gone to extraordinary lengths to preserve the mid-engine handling character that defines the Cayman experience. The battery pack is concentrated as low and centrally as possible, with the rear motor positioned where the flat-six once sat. The result, according to Porsche's head of dynamics Andreas Preuninger, is a center of gravity that is actually lower than the current 718.

Range is projected at 310 miles on the WLTP cycle, with 800-volt architecture enabling 270kW DC fast charging. A 10 to 80 percent charge will take approximately 22 minutes. Porsche is also introducing a new two-speed rear transmission, similar to the Taycan's setup but lighter and more compact.

The electric Cayman will arrive in late 2027 with a starting price expected around $78,000. A stripped-back T variant and a range-topping GTS are already confirmed for 2028, ensuring the Cayman lineup remains a cornerstone of Porsche's sports car portfolio.

title: Ford Mustang Dark Horse S650 Track Pack Revealed
excerpt: Ford adds a factory track pack to the Dark Horse with carbon ceramic brakes, adjustable aero, and 10 more horsepower from the 5.0L Coyote V8.
content:
Ford Performance has unveiled the most track-focused Mustang you can buy from the factory: the Dark Horse Track Pack. Building on the already potent Dark Horse platform, this new option package pushes the S650 generation firmly into serious performance car territory with a suite of upgrades that justify the additional spend.

Under the hood, the 5.0-liter Coyote V8 gains a revised airbox and freer-flowing exhaust headers, lifting output to 510 horsepower and 428 lb-ft of torque. The Tremec six-speed manual is the only transmission available, a deliberate choice that earned a standing ovation when announced at the Detroit reveal event.

The chassis upgrades are where things get really interesting. Carbon ceramic brake rotors from Brembo replace the standard steel units, saving 42 pounds of unsprung weight. The MagneRide 4.0 dampers receive track-specific calibration, and a new adjustable carbon fiber rear wing generates measurable downforce above 80 mph. Michelin Pilot Sport Cup 2 R tires are fitted as standard.

Priced at $72,990, the Track Pack occupies a fascinating space in the market, undercutting the Porsche 911 Carrera by a significant margin while offering comparable lap times at most circuits. Ford says production will be limited to 2,500 units for the 2026 model year.

title: Nissan Z NISMO Gets 420hp Twin-Turbo V6 for 2026
excerpt: The hottest Z yet arrives with 420hp, a limited-slip diff, Recaro seats, and NISMO-tuned suspension. Only 1,000 units for the US market.
content:
Nissan has finally delivered the car enthusiasts have been demanding since the Z Proto concept debuted years ago. The 2026 Z NISMO takes the already capable twin-turbocharged VR30DDTT 3.0-liter V6 and turns everything up a notch, extracting 420 horsepower and 384 lb-ft of torque through revised turbo internals, a larger intercooler, and optimized boost mapping.

The NISMO treatment extends well beyond the engine bay. A mechanical limited-slip differential replaces the standard open unit, transforming corner exit traction. NISMO-tuned Bilstein dampers pair with stiffer springs and thicker anti-roll bars to deliver the sharp, responsive handling that the base Z sometimes lacks. The steering rack ratio has been quickened slightly, and the brake pedal feel has been firmed up considerably.

Visually, the Z NISMO wears a subtle but purposeful body kit with a deeper front splitter, wider side skirts, and a ducktail rear spoiler, all finished in dry carbon fiber. The signature NISMO red accent stripe runs along the roofline and down the C-pillar. Inside, Recaro bucket seats in Alcantara and leather grip you tightly, flanked by a NISMO-specific flat-bottom steering wheel.

With only 1,000 units allocated for the US market at $55,490 each, the Z NISMO is positioned as a modern collector's piece. Nissan expects allocation requests to far exceed supply, so prospective buyers are encouraged to contact their dealers immediately.

title: Lamborghini Revuelto First Drive: 1,001hp of Hybrid Fury
excerpt: We drive Lamborghini's V12 hybrid hypercar on road and track. The Revuelto is violent, theatrical, and absolutely magnificent.
content:
The Lamborghini Revuelto is the most important car Sant'Agata has ever built. It is the first series-production Lamborghini with electrification, the first with an all-new V12 since the Murcielago, and the car that must prove the raging bull can evolve without losing its soul. After two days and 400 miles across Sardinia, we can confirm: the soul is not only intact, it has been supercharged.

The numbers are staggering. A 6.5-liter naturally aspirated V12 producing 825 horsepower is supplemented by three electric motors adding another 176 horsepower, for a combined system output of 1,001hp. The new eight-speed dual-clutch gearbox replaces the old single-clutch unit, and the difference in refinement is night and day. Shifts are savage in Corsa mode, seamless in Strada. The 0-to-62 sprint takes 2.5 seconds, and the sensation of acceleration never seems to plateau.

On the mountain roads above Cagliari, the Revuelto reveals its true genius. The front axle, driven by two electric motors, provides a level of turn-in precision that no previous Lamborghini has offered. The rear-steer system works in concert to shrink the car's footprint through tight switchbacks, and the carbon fiber monocoque chassis provides a level of structural rigidity that translates directly into confidence-inspiring feedback through the steering wheel.

At $608,358 before options, the Revuelto is not cheap. But in a world of sanitized, turbocharged hypercars that prioritize lap times over emotion, Lamborghini has built something refreshingly unapologetic. The V12 screams to 9,500 rpm, the exhaust crackles and pops on the overrun, and every drive feels like an event. This is what a supercar should be.

title: BMW M2 CS Leaked with 523hp and Manual Gearbox
excerpt: Internal documents reveal BMW's lightweight M2 CS will pack 523hp from its twin-turbo straight-six, a six-speed manual, and carbon bucket seats.
content:
Leaked internal documents from BMW M GmbH have confirmed what the rumor mill has been churning for months: the M2 CS is coming, and it is going to be an absolute weapon. According to the documents, first obtained by a German automotive forum and verified by our sources in Munich, the S58 twin-turbocharged 3.0-liter inline-six will be tuned to produce 523 horsepower and 443 lb-ft of torque.

Critically, the M2 CS will be offered exclusively with a six-speed manual transmission. BMW M's decision to drop the eight-speed automatic option is a bold statement of intent, positioning the CS as a purist's machine in an era of paddle shifters and dual-clutch units. A revised short-throw shifter with a weighted knob is expected to address the somewhat vague feel of the standard M2's manual.

Weight reduction is central to the CS formula. Carbon fiber reinforced plastic replaces steel for the hood, roof, and trunk lid. The rear seats are deleted entirely, replaced by a carpeted storage area and a half roll cage. Carbon bucket seats from the M4 CSL are fitted up front. Total weight savings are estimated at 85 kilograms over the standard M2, bringing the curb weight down to approximately 1,555 kg.

BMW is expected to announce the M2 CS officially at the Goodwood Festival of Speed this July, with deliveries beginning in Q4 2026. Pricing is rumored at approximately $95,000, placing it squarely against the Porsche 718 Cayman GT4 RS.

title: Tesla Roadster Sets Blistering Nurburgring Lap in Secret Test
excerpt: A camouflaged Tesla Roadster was spotted completing a sub-7-minute Nurburgring lap, suggesting performance figures even beyond Musk's bold claims.
content:
A heavily camouflaged Tesla Roadster has been spotted completing what multiple trackside observers estimate was a sub-7-minute Nordschleife lap during a secretive late-night testing session at the Nurburgring. If confirmed, that time would place the Roadster among the fastest production cars ever to lap the legendary circuit.

Footage captured by a local photographer shows the Roadster carrying significant aero modifications not seen on previously leaked prototypes, including a large fixed rear wing, aggressive front canards, and what appears to be an active underbody diffuser system. The car was accompanied by a convoy of Tesla engineering vehicles and completed approximately eight laps over several hours.

Tesla has remained characteristically tight-lipped about the Roadster's development timeline, but the Nurburgring appearance suggests the program is further along than many skeptics believed. The production Roadster is expected to feature a tri-motor powertrain producing over 1,000 horsepower, with a 200 kWh battery pack providing a claimed range in excess of 600 miles.

The timing of this test is notable. Rimac recently set an EV production car record at the Ring with the Nevera, and Porsche's Mission X concept has been seen testing there as well. The electric hypercar segment is quickly becoming the new battleground for manufacturer bragging rights, and Tesla clearly intends to compete at the highest level.

title: Honda Civic Type R Gets Mid-Cycle Refresh with Active Aero
excerpt: Honda's hot hatch gains active aero elements, revised turbo mapping, and a limited-edition Championship White colorway for 2026.
content:
Honda has revealed the mid-cycle refresh for the FL5 Civic Type R, and while the changes might appear subtle at first glance, they represent meaningful improvements to an already outstanding performance car. The headline addition is an active rear wing that adjusts its angle of attack based on speed and driving mode, reducing drag at highway speeds while maximizing downforce during track use.

The 2.0-liter VTEC Turbo four-cylinder retains its 315 horsepower output, but revised turbo mapping delivers improved mid-range response between 3,000 and 5,000 rpm, addressing the only dynamic criticism leveled at the original FL5. The six-speed manual gearbox, widely regarded as the best in the business, carries over unchanged, as does the helical limited-slip differential.

Suspension tuning has been refined based on data collected from Type R owners who participate in track days. The adaptive dampers now offer a wider spread between Comfort and +R modes, making the car more livable on broken surfaces without sacrificing composure at the limit. New 20-inch forged alloy wheels save 1.2 kg per corner of unsprung weight.

A limited-edition Championship White launch colorway pays homage to the original EK9 Civic Type R, complete with a red Honda badge, red Brembo calipers, and a numbered plaque on the center console. Only 500 units will be produced globally. Standard models start at $44,295 and are available to order now.

title: 2026 F1 Regulations: What the New Rules Mean for Racing
excerpt: With simplified aero, more powerful electric motors, and active aerodynamics, the 2026 F1 season promises to reshape the competitive order entirely.
content:
The 2026 Formula 1 season is just months away, and the new technical regulations represent the most significant shake-up in the sport's recent history. Gone are the complex bargeboards and intricate floor tunnels of the current generation. In their place, the FIA has mandated radically simplified bodywork combined with active aerodynamic elements on the front and rear wings.

The power unit regulations are equally transformative. The MGU-H, the controversial heat energy recovery system that drove up costs and deterred new manufacturers, has been eliminated. In its place, the MGU-K has been dramatically upscaled, with the electric motor now contributing approximately 350 horsepower alongside the 1.6-liter V6 turbo's estimated 550 horsepower. The result is a roughly equal split between combustion and electric power for the first time in F1 history.

For the teams, these regulations represent both an opportunity and a threat. The massive aero rule changes are expected to compress the performance gap between front-runners and midfield teams, at least initially. Ford's return as a power unit partner with Red Bull, Audi's entry as a full factory team taking over Sauber, and Honda's continued partnership with Aston Martin mean the 2026 grid will feature six distinct power unit manufacturers for the first time in over a decade.

The pre-season testing data suggests we could be in for one of the most competitive seasons in years. Multiple team principals have privately admitted they have no idea where they stand relative to the competition, a sentiment not heard in the paddock since the turbo-hybrid era began in 2014.

title: Dodge Charger Daytona EV: Can an Electric Muscle Car Work?
excerpt: We spend a week with the electric Charger Daytona to find out if Dodge's synthesized exhaust and brutish styling can satisfy muscle car loyalists.
content:
The Dodge Charger Daytona is the car that was never supposed to exist, at least according to the internet commenters who declared muscle cars dead the moment Stellantis confirmed the electric transition. After a week behind the wheel of the 670-horsepower Scat Pack variant, we can report that the muscle car is alive, just fundamentally different.

Let us address the elephant in the room first: the Fratzonic Chambered Exhaust system. Dodge's much-ridiculed synthesized exhaust produces a 126-decibel roar through a tuning chamber at the rear of the car, and in person, it is significantly more convincing than any YouTube video suggests. It does not sound like a Hemi. It sounds like something new, something industrial and aggressive that suits the Daytona's brutalist design language.

Dynamically, the Charger Daytona is a revelation. The 800-volt Banshee propulsion system delivers its 670 horsepower with a ferocity that makes the old 6.4-liter Hemi feel sluggish by comparison. The eRupt multi-speed transmission simulates gear changes with genuine mechanical jolts, adding a rhythm to acceleration that pure single-speed EVs lack entirely. Through corners, the low center of gravity and near-perfect 50/50 weight distribution expose just how compromised the old rear-heavy, live-axle platform really was.

At $59,595, the Charger Daytona Scat Pack undercuts its Hellcat predecessor while offering objectively superior performance in every measurable metric. Whether that is enough to win over the faithful remains to be seen, but Dodge deserves credit for refusing to build just another anonymous electric sedan.

title: McLaren 750S vs Ferrari 296 GTB: Supercar Shootout
excerpt: Two mid-engine titans go head to head on road and track. The 750S brings raw mechanical purity while the 296 GTB counters with hybrid sophistication.
content:
There is a philosophical divide at the heart of the modern supercar world, and these two machines sit squarely on opposite sides of it. The McLaren 750S is a monument to lightness, mechanical purity, and hydraulic steering feel. The Ferrari 296 GTB is a technological tour de force that combines a twin-turbo V6 with an electric motor to deliver 819 combined horsepower. Both cost roughly the same. Both are spectacular. But they could not be more different to drive.

On the road, the McLaren is the more engaging companion. Its electrohydraulic steering is the last of its kind in the supercar segment, and it floods your palms with information about road surface, tire load, and grip level. The 4.0-liter twin-turbo V8 delivers its 750 horsepower with a linearity that belies its forced induction, and the seven-speed dual-clutch gearbox fires off shifts with surgical precision. At 1,277 kg dry, the 750S feels like a car from another era, delicate and responsive in a way that heavier rivals simply cannot match.

The Ferrari takes a different approach entirely. The 296 GTB's 3.0-liter V6 is a masterpiece of internal combustion engineering, revving to 8,500 rpm with a top-end fury reminiscent of the old 458. The hybrid system fills in the torque curve seamlessly, and in combined mode the acceleration is relentless. The chassis is supremely competent, with rear-axle steering providing an agility that makes the car feel far smaller than its dimensions suggest.

On our instrumented test at Anglesey Circuit, the Ferrari was 1.3 seconds quicker per lap. On the road, three out of four testers preferred the McLaren. The numbers say Ferrari. The heart says McLaren. We say buy whichever one makes you grin wider on the test drive.

title: Le Mans 2026 Preview: Toyota, Ferrari, Porsche, and the Fight for Glory
excerpt: With Lamborghini and Alpine joining the grid, the 2026 WEC Hypercar class is shaping up to be the most competitive endurance racing season in decades.
content:
The 2026 FIA World Endurance Championship season opener at Qatar confirmed what the pre-season hype had promised: the Hypercar class is absolutely stacked. With Toyota, Ferrari, Porsche, Peugeot, BMW, Cadillac, Lamborghini, and Alpine all fielding factory or supported entries, the grid is deeper in manufacturer talent than at any point since the Group C era of the late 1980s.

Toyota enters its tenth season of top-class prototype racing with a significantly revised GR010 Hybrid. The Japanese manufacturer spent heavily during the off-season, focusing on tire management and pit stop strategy after being outgunned by Ferrari's superior race pace at Le Mans last year. Their driver lineup of Buemi, Hartley, and Hirakawa remains unchanged and is widely regarded as the strongest on the grid.

Ferrari's 499P, the defending Le Mans champion, returns with evolutionary updates rather than revolutionary changes. The Maranello team has learned that consistency wins endurance races, and their pre-season reliability record was flawless across 30,000 kilometers of testing. Porsche, meanwhile, has finally sorted the teething issues that plagued the 963 LMDh in its debut seasons and looked genuinely quick in Qatar qualifying.

The wildcards to watch are Lamborghini and Alpine. Lamborghini's SC63 showed flashes of pace in its debut year but was hampered by reliability gremlins. The Squadra Corse engineers have had a full winter to address those issues. Alpine's A424 is an elegant machine that former Peugeot technical director Olivier Jansonnie has developed from scratch. Both teams are realistic about their chances at Le Mans, targeting podiums rather than outright victory, but stranger things have happened at La Sarthe.
